todaysPlan.reason: Self-identified math gap never formally assessed across 25+ sessions; advanced addition fluency (16/16 correct, 100%) indicates readiness for multiplicative reasoning via familiar wrestling contexts
todaysPlan.method: Conceptual introduction through wrestling word problems: '3 matches × 2 points each = ? total points.' Use manipulatives or visual arrays if needed. Celebrate with Japanese (すごい, やった) and wrestling metaphors ('pin the problem').
todaysPlan.source: Profile math gaps (subtraction, multiplication, division); psychologist reports flagging multiplication as critical priority

todaysPlan.reason: Reading fluency 100% but comprehension remains literal only ('she went boom, but she was safe'); needs depth before Constitution reading or complex Japanese texts
todaysPlan.method: Action/adventure text with 'why' and 'what if' questions: 'Why was the ninja in danger?' 'How did she outsmart them?' Match her chess/wrestling analytical brain with strategy analysis.
todaysPlan.source: Reading sessions 4/3, 4/4 showing literal comprehension only; parent goal of Constitution reading

todaysPlan.reason: Pattern-based spelling mastered (classroom baseline 100%); maintain as brief warm-up aligned with current homework cycle
todaysPlan.method: 5-word maximum from homework list (faster/fastest, slower/slowest, colder/coldest). Competitive framing: 'Beat your perfect streak!' Then pivot to her choice.

todaysPlan.reason: Mastery-level foundation (100% accuracy) serves as confidence-builder before harder multiplication work
todaysPlan.method: Rapid-fire 5-problem set mixing single-digit and crossing-10 facts. Celebrate consecutive correct streak. Use as energizer before multiplication introduction.
skipConditions: Reina requests immediate challenge without warm-up
Previous session showed sustained focus without warm-up needed

todaysPlan.reason: Advanced vocabulary claimed in profile but never formally assessed across 25+ sessions; required baseline before Japanese kanji/katakana work (June 2026 trip)
todaysPlan.method: CELF-5 Word Definitions subtest protocol: 'What does [word] mean?' Start with grade-level words (animal, color, story) then advance to academic vocabulary (multiply, strategy, champion). Record definitional depth vs. breadth.
todaysPlan.source: Flagged 25 times in clinical notes; parent goal Japanese prep requires vocabulary baseline

todaysPlan.reason: No narrative production or sentence generation under demand across any session; relevant for Japanese composition and written math explanations
todaysPlan.method: CELF-5 Formulated Sentences subtest protocol: Present word and ask child to create sentence. Start with familiar words (wrestler, match, points) then advance to academic terms (multiply, product, strategy).
todaysPlan.source: Flagged 21 times in clinical notes; needed before bilingual curriculum expansion
skipConditions: Energy level low after multiplication and reading work

childProfile: Reina is an 8-year-old academically advanced learner (year ahead of peers) who thrives on challenge, competition, and speed. She is bilingual (English/Japanese), a competitive wrestler, and consistently scores 100% on spelling tests. She masters new concepts through applied contexts (store-game 86% accuracy) and competitive framing (math facts 100%, riddles 15+/15 correct) but disengages from mechanical drill (worksheet coins 26% accuracy). She has strong nonverbal reasoning, self-corrects errors, and self-advocates for harder material when bored. Wrestling metaphors, Japanese celebration (すごい, やった), and fast-paced energy matching work best. She gets frustrated with slow pacing, baby talk, or tasks that feel too easy. Her self-identified math gaps are subtraction, multiplication, and division—multiplication has never been formally assessed despite 25+ sessions.
stopAfter: End session naturally when Reina completes multiplication introduction, inferential reading work, and at least one CELF-5 probe (Word Definitions priority), OR when she self-regulates exit on a winning streak. Typical session length 25-35 minutes. Stop immediately if she says she's done or requests Space Invaders—respect her boundary and celebrate wins before closing.
rewardPolicy: Reina earns games/breaks through mastery and sustained effort, not as bribes. After completing required activities (multiplication intro, inferential reading, Word Definitions probe) at 80%+ engagement, offer Space Invaders or choice of next challenge. Use competitive framing: 'You pinned that multiplication problem—champion's choice: harder math or game break?' Match rewards to her identity (wrestler, smart kid who loves challenge). Japanese celebration (すごい, やった) and acknowledgment of her intelligence are intrinsic motivators—external rewards secondary.
